summary: Joel announces the release of FogBugz 7, describing how the team recovered from a disorganized development process by resetting plans and writing a focused vision statement in August 2008. The vision centered on three pillars: 'fruity treats' (small customer-requested improvements), customization (a plugin architecture), and 'supersonics' (performance improvements via .NET/Mono). The team used Evidence Based Scheduling to ship on time within one year. Joel credits the team rather than himself and highlights how a concise, shared vision statement made prioritization easy by defining what was out of scope as much as what was in.
key_points: A concise vision statement that defines what's NOT in the product is as valuable as defining what IS in it, enabling sharp prioritization across a year-long project
Evidence Based Scheduling worked well on a large one-year project, giving the team confidence to both cut features and add new ones like Scrum support
The team recovered from a failed strategy of splitting FogBugz into two products by merging teams and refocusing on a single, comprehensible release
The plugin architecture replaced the unsatisfying answer of 'you get the source code, so you can modify it' with a sustainable extensibility model
Joel positions himself as increasingly removed from hands-on development, crediting the growing professional team for the product's quality
money_quotes: One of the best things we did as a development team was to write a short, concise, comprehensible vision statement that got everybody exactly on the same page about what we were going to do over the course of a year.
The vision statement made it easy to prioritize. Instead of just telling us what was in the product, it also gave us a way to know what was out.
No more HALF-ASSED features (I'm looking at you, logo customization in FogBugz 6.1).
If it's NOT ON THAT LIST it's NOT IN THE PRODUCT. Get used to it.
They know perfectly well that if they modify our source code, terribobble tragedies will occur the minute we release a service pack.

I wish I could take more credit for it, but the truth is, Fog Creek has grown. We have a very professional team with testers, program managers, and developers, and I just sort of sit here agog at what a brilliant job they're doing.
I'm just the Michael Scott character who wastes everybody's time whenever I venture out of my office.
key_insight: A sharp, concise vision statement that explicitly defines what's out of scope is the most effective tool for keeping a development team aligned and on schedule over a long project.
